nav.rulesStandard: Sign language standard and sign variants
rules.standard.title: Sign language standard and sign variants
rules.standard.lead: About the unified sign standard and regional / usage variants.
rules.videoPlaceholder: An explanatory video will be added soon.
rules.standard.s1.title: Standardising sign language
rules.standard.s1.p1: Sign language is a living language that constantly changes, enriches and develops through the communication of deaf people. Across the world it has been common to standardise the sign language used by the deaf community by linking just one chosen sign — out of many variants — to a single word in order to build a dictionary.
rules.standard.s1.p2: Standardising a sign language means selecting only one sign from the many same-meaning variants that deaf people use and tying it to a word, while disregarding the other variants — or tying each remaining variant to a different word. A written language with grammatical structure and spelling rules can be standardised, but spoken languages and sign languages around the world cannot.
rules.standard.s1.p3: Let us explain with an example. From the near-synonyms widely used in speech — “эхнэр, авгай, гэргий” (wife) — you cannot standardise on just the single word “эхнэр” and leave the others unmarked and unused.
rules.standard.s1.p4: In the same way, the Mongolian sign for “мартах” (to forget) has several close-meaning variants, and you cannot pick only one as the standard sign for “forget” while dropping the rest. Deaf people use the many variants of this sign according to context. Nor can each variant be tied to a different word: though they differ as signs, they all carry the same meaning — “to forget”.
rules.standard.s2.title: Standardisation and language rights
rules.standard.s2.p1: When children who learned a standardised sign language — together with their parents and teachers — struggle to understand members of the deaf community, it shows how little value standardisation holds. Standardising a sign language is, in effect, a form of suppressing deaf people's language rights. Article 21 of the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities obliges signatory states to recognise and promote sign languages. This expresses the idea of accepting and disseminating the everyday variants of signs that deaf people commonly use.
rules.standard.s3.title: What is a sign variant?
rules.standard.s3.p1: A sign variant means expressing a single word with several different signs. Different, mutually distinct variants arise depending on the age and sex of community members and where they live. An example of a word with many same-meaning variants is “хэлэх” (to say): its sign is used in the following three different variants, yet the meaning is the same. The sign for “заах” (to point/teach) likewise has several variants, and “амархан” (easy) can be signed in three different ways with the same meaning — all sign variants.
rules.standard.s3.p2: Of course, you cannot use any of these variants arbitrarily. You choose the variant that fits the topic, event, time, spatial location and intended meaning. To learn to use variants correctly, you can enter the deaf community and carefully observe and converse in their sign language, as well as take sign-language classes. From the sign variants and example sentences in this Mongolian Sign Language online dictionary, you can study and learn the language.
rules.structure.title: Sign structure
rules.structure.lead: How a sign is built — the five parameters: handshape, location, movement, palm orientation and non-manual expression.
rules.structure.s1.title: How a sign is built
rules.structure.s1.p1: In spoken language, sounds and phonemes combine to form words. For example, the three sounds “sh”, “a”, “r” combine into “шар” (yellow). In sign language, a single sign is built from the combination of five hand parameters: handshape, location, movement, palm orientation and non-manual expression.
rules.structure.s1.p2: For example, with the handshape shown here, the location touching the cheek, a straight movement, the palm facing inward, and a non-manual expression of pursed lips, the sign “ШАР” (yellow) is formed.
rules.structure.s2.title: The five parameters
rules.structure.s2.p1: The first parameter is handshape. Each sign is expressed with one or more handshapes. Signs made with a single handshape include “БАЯРЛАЛАА” (thank you), “ГОМДОХ” (to be hurt) and “ТУСЛАХ” (to help). Signs made with two or more handshapes include “ЦЭЦЭРЛЭГ” (kindergarten), “НАР” (sun) and “ДЭЭД СУРГУУЛЬ” (university).
rules.structure.s2.p2: Location is the place on or near the body where a sign is made. For example, “БОДОХ” (to think) is at the temple, “ДЭВТЭР” (notebook) on the hand, “ТЭНГЭР” (sky) above the head, “ЗААХ” (to point) in front of the body, and “СЭТГЭЛ” (feeling) at the chest. Each sign has its own location.
rules.structure.s2.p3: Sign language is a language of movement. Each sign is expressed with a particular movement. For example, “БАЙНА” (there is) moves straight downward, “ДУГУЙ” (wheel/round) moves in a circle, “ГОЛ” (river) moves in a wavy line, and “МАГАДГҮЙ” (maybe) is expressed with a trembling movement.
rules.structure.s2.p4: Palm orientation is the direction the palm faces. Whether the hand is closed or open, it must face a specific direction. For example, the palm of “МИНИЙ” (mine) faces inward, “АХЛАГЧ” (leader) faces left and down, and in “УУЛЗАХ” (to meet) the right palm faces left while the left palm faces right. Each sign has its own palm orientation.
rules.structure.s2.p5: Non-manual expression includes facial expression, body movement, head movement, gaze and lip movements. Signing something without non-manual expression makes its meaning hard to understand. Used together with a sign, it makes the meaning clear. For example, “УУРЛАХ” (to be angry) and “БАЯРЛАХ” (to be happy) look unclear without non-manual expression, but become very clear with it.
rules.structure.s3.title: One wrong parameter changes the meaning
rules.structure.s3.p1: If any one of the five parameters is performed incorrectly, the meaning of the sign changes. Watch the video below to see how it changes and what consequences follow.
rules.structure.s3.p2: 1. Handshape. “Ах” (older brother) is made with one shape and “хар” (black) with another. Meaning to ask “What is your brother doing?”, signing the handshape of “ах” incorrectly turns it into the confusing question “What is black doing?”. A wrong handshape alone gives the sign a different meaning, so the handshape of what you mean must be performed correctly.
rules.structure.s3.p3: 2. Location. “Өвөө” (grandfather) and “эмээ” (grandmother) share handshape, movement and palm orientation, but differ in location and non-manual expression. “Өвөө” is signed beside the forehead, “эмээ” at the chin. Asking “Is your grandfather well?” at the wrong location — mouthing “өвөө” while the sign is “эмээ” — causes confusion. A sign's location cannot be arbitrary.
rules.structure.s3.p4: 3. Movement. “Тэмцээн” (competition) and “баян” (rich) share handshape and location but differ in movement. “Тэмцээн” repeats twice at one location, while “баян” moves straight upward. Signing “Shall we watch the competition together?” with the wrong movement turns it into the meaningless “Shall we watch rich together?”. Without the correct movement, you risk being misunderstood.
rules.structure.s3.p5: 4. Palm orientation. “Байна” (there is) and “өгөөч” (please give) share handshape, location and movement, differing only in palm orientation. The right palm of “байна” faces left, while that of “өгөөч” faces up. Signing “Please give me your phone” with a changed palm orientation is misread as “There is a phone”, so the two people misunderstand each other. Be mindful — palm orientation changes the meaning.
rules.structure.s3.p6: 5. Non-manual expression. Some signs share handshape, movement, location and palm orientation, and are told apart by non-manual expression. “Гарах” (to come out / appear) and “балрах, сүйрэх” (to collapse, fail) differ only in non-manual expression. Showing off “A new phone like this has come out” is understood by the other person as “the phone has broken”, because “гарах” was signed with the non-manual expression of “сүйрэх, балрах”. A tiny change in non-manual expression causes misunderstanding, so it deserves attention.
rules.mouthing.title: Mouthing and lip shapes
rules.mouthing.lead: How mouthing and lip shapes help distinguish and clarify the meaning of a sign.
rules.mouthing.s1.title: Non-manual expression and mouthing
rules.mouthing.s1.p1: A single sign is built from five parameters: handshape, location, movement, palm orientation and non-manual expression. The first four involve the hands; the fifth, non-manual expression, includes facial expression, body movement, head movement, gaze, and mouthing and lip shape. For example, “ШИНЭ” (new) and “ЦЭВЭР” (clean) have exactly the same handshape, location, movement and palm orientation, and differ only in non-manual expression. The lips of “ШИНЭ” form the shape of the letter “sh”, while “ЦЭВЭР” is signed with narrowed eyes and a tightened mouth.
rules.mouthing.s1.p2: In another example, “ГАДААД” (foreign), “АРГА” (method) and “БОДОХ” (to think) share the same first four parameters but differ in non-manual expression: “ГАДААД” by a mouth shape, “АРГА” by a head shake, and “БОДОХ” by lowered brows and narrowed eyes — each expressing a completely different word.
rules.mouthing.s2.title: Three types of mouthing
rules.mouthing.s2.p1: Among the elements of non-manual expression, let us explain mouthing and lip shape in more detail. Mouthing is grouped into three types: (1) mouthing that expresses the whole word, (2) mouthing that expresses part of the word, and (3) a mouth or lip movement that depicts the sign's meaning independent of the word.
rules.mouthing.s2.p2: For example, signs with whole-word mouthing: “ААВ” (father), “ЭЭЖ” (mother), “НОМ” (book). Signs with partial mouthing: “ГАЗАР” (place), “МЭДЭХ” (to know), “ОЙЛГОХ” (to understand). Signs with a mouth/lip movement that depicts meaning: “ТААРУУХАН” (mediocre), “ДАЖГҮЙ” (not bad), “ТУРАНХАЙ” (thin).
rules.mouthing.s2.p3: “ХАЙРТАЙ” (to love) can be expressed with all three types of mouthing. The first mouths the whole word “хайртай”, the next mouths only part, “хай”, and the last uses a “tightened” lip shape. All of these variants may be used.
rules.mouthing.s2.p4: “ГОМДОХ” (to be hurt) can likewise be shown three ways: the first mouths the whole word “гомдох”, the second mouths part, “гом…”, and the third does not mouth the word but uses a “drooped, pursed” lip shape. All three are variants of the same-meaning sign.
rules.mouthing.s2.p5: However, not every sign can be expressed in all three types at once. For example, “САЛХИ” (wind), made with a “blowing” lip movement, cannot be mouthed as the whole word “салхи”. Likewise “САРМАГЧИН” (monkey), made with the tongue and pouted lips, cannot be mouthed as “сармагчин”. Some signs are expressed with only one type of mouthing.
rules.mouthing.s3.title: Using mouthing in sentences
rules.mouthing.s3.p1: Watch the video to see how the three types of mouthing — whole-word, partial, and mouth/lip movement — are all used in a sentence expressed in Mongolian Sign Language. Example sentence: “Before the upcoming basketball competition, our team prepared well.”
rules.mouthing.s3.p2: Mouthing every word one by one while signing a sentence is not “Mongolian Sign Language”. Fitting the right one of the three mouthing types to each sign is “true” Mongolian Sign Language. Learn to recognise and correctly use mouth and lip shapes and movements. You can study from the example sentences in this “Mongolian Sign Language online dictionary”.
landing.hero.lead: Search Mongolian Sign Language, watch it on video, learn through games, and contribute new signs yourself — all in one place.
landing.features.title: Everything you need to learn, in one place
landing.features.lead: A video-first dictionary, regional variants, game-based learning, and an accessibility-first design.
landing.features.f1.title: Video dictionary
landing.features.f1.body: Every sign on video. Filter by topic, hand count and region, and slow it down to 0.5×.
landing.features.f2.title: Regional variants
landing.features.f2.body: Compare the many variants of a single word side by side, by region.
landing.features.f3.title: Learn through games
landing.features.f3.body: Daily quizzes, streaks and badges. Practice feels like play — keep your streak and keep progressing.
landing.features.f4.title: Mouthing
landing.features.f4.body: We show the mouth-shape pattern that distinguishes similar signs, right alongside the sign.
landing.features.f5.title: Accessibility first
landing.features.f5.body: WCAG AA contrast, keyboard focus, motion control and dark mode — built in from the ground up.
landing.features.f6.title: Community contribution
landing.features.f6.body: Users submit their signs, teachers review and publish — the dictionary grows together.
landing.how.title: Three simple steps
landing.how.s1.title: Search
landing.how.s1.body: Type a word and the sign and its variants appear on video.
landing.how.s2.title: Watch & learn
landing.how.s2.body: Watch it slowed down, repeat the mouthing, and reinforce it with games.
landing.how.s3.title: Contribute
landing.how.s3.body: Submit a sign you know — a teacher reviews it and adds it to the public dictionary.
landing.dict.title: Every sign on video — one word, many variants
landing.dict.lead: Hover over a card to trace the hand path.
landing.dict.noResult: no matching signs found. Try a different word or topic.
landing.dict.empty: No signs have been added to the dictionary yet.
landing.learn.title: A little every day — keep your streak alive
landing.learn.weekdays: Mo Tu We Th Fr Sa Su
landing.learn.dailyBody: Guess today's sign. How many of 3 tries will you get right?
landing.learn.badgesBody: Prove what you've learned with badges.
landing.testi.title: What the community says
landing.testi.q1: “My child and I now learn three new signs every evening. The videos are clear, and the slow-motion is a huge help.”
landing.testi.q1.name: Bolormaa
landing.testi.q2: “Showing regional variants side by side is invaluable for teaching. The community submissions are well moderated.”
landing.testi.q2.name: Ganbat
landing.contribute.title: The dictionary is built by the community
landing.contribute.body: Know a sign that's missing from the dictionary? Submit your video — teachers review and publish it. Every submission is stored with consent.
landing.contribute.s1.title: Write & submit
landing.contribute.s2.title: Duplicate check
landing.contribute.s3.title: Teacher reviews
landing.contribute.s3.body: If approved, it's added to the dictionary
landing.cta.title: Learn Mongolian Sign Language through movement
landing.cta.body: Sign up free and join the dictionary, games and community. From any device.
landing.foot.tagline: “A sign is movement.” An open, community-driven platform for Mongolian Sign Language.
